nativeRange.habitat: Deep rocky lakeshores and open water of large rift lakes; found along boulder- and rock-strewn shorelines and deeper benthic zones of Lake Managua and Lake Nicaragua

decorPreferences: large flat rocks and boulders for territory demarcation
heavy substrate anchoring — all decor must be secured or removed (destructive digger)
bare-bottom or coarse gravel (fine sand will be rearranged constantly)
minimal or no live plants (will be uprooted and destroyed)
robust external canister filtration — high bioload species

diet.notes: Accepts a broad range of foods: high-quality large cichlid pellets as staple; supplement with earthworms, raw prawns, mussels, and frozen bloodworm. Occasional vegetable matter (blanched courgette, spirulina wafers) beneficial. Feed sparingly to avoid obesity and excessive waste; large portions 1–2 times daily. Avoid feeder goldfish (thiaminase, parasite vector) and excessive beef heart (fatty, associated with Hole-in-the-Head disease).

avoidWith: virtually all other fish — will attack, injure, or kill most tankmates
conspecifics (extreme intraspecific aggression outside spawning season)
Midas Cichlid (Amphilophus citrinellus, fw-fish-409) — hybridises readily, producing Flowerhorn lineage; keep apart unless intentional hybridisation is goal
any small or slender fish that fit in the mouth

imageCandidates.notes: Adult male showing orange colouration and fleshy hypertrophied lips; clear full-body lateral view. Good representative image.
FishBase species image — check current availability and licence before use.

summary: The Red Devil is a large, extremely aggressive Central American cichlid from Lakes Managua and Nicaragua. Adults reach 38 cm and develop a fleshy lip swelling and a pronounced cranial nuchal hump in males. Suitable only for specialist single-specimen setups of 500 L or more. One of the parent species of the Flowerhorn hybrid.
careNotes: The Red Devil (Amphilophus labiatus) is one of the most pugnacious cichlids available in the trade and should not be underestimated. Adults commonly reach 35–38 cm total length and have a powerful jaw capable of moving rocks and destroying all but the most robust aquarium equipment. A single-specimen tank of at least 500 L with a footprint of 180 cm or more is the minimum responsible setup; no tankmates are recommended. Even established mated pairs must be watched closely, as males will kill females outside spawning readiness. Heavy-duty external canister filtration rated for at least twice the tank volume is essential given the species’ substantial bioload. The Red Devil can be distinguished from the closely related Midas Cichlid (A. citrinellus, fw-fish-409) by its characteristically hypertrophied, fleshy lips — a prominent feature that becomes more pronounced with age. Adult males develop a large cranial nuchal hump ("kok"), particularly conspicuous in dominant individuals. Coloration in the hobby ranges from orange-red to yellow and white; some wild-type fish retain a grey-brown juvenile pattern. The species is native exclusively to Lake Managua and Lake Nicaragua in Nicaragua, where it inhabits rocky shorelines and open-water benthic zones. Water should be maintained at moderate hardness and neutral-to-alkaline pH reflecting the alkaline conditions of these volcanic crater-influenced lakes. Perform large (30–40%) weekly water changes to manage nitrate accumulation. Decor must be thoroughly secured or removed entirely, as the Red Devil will rearrange the aquascape destructively. Live plants are not viable; use robust artificial plants anchored with weights if desired. The species is highly intelligent and individual fish often become responsive to their keeper, which contributes to its enduring popularity despite the specialist demands.
breedingNotes: Biparental substrate spawner. A compatible, size-matched pair will excavate a spawning pit in the substrate and deposit up to 700 pale adhesive eggs on a flat cleaned rock surface. Both parents guard eggs and wriggling larvae aggressively. Fry become free-swimming after approximately 4–5 days at 26°C and can be raised on baby brine shrimp and finely crushed cichlid pellets. Separating the pair promptly after fry become independent is strongly advised as males frequently attack females when parental motivation wanes. Hybridisation with A. citrinellus (Midas Cichlid) occurs readily and produces fertile offspring that form part of the Flowerhorn hybrid lineage; strict isolation of pure-form individuals is recommended unless hybridisation is the deliberate aim.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Amphilophus labiatus
primary.notes: Primary reference. Provided adult size (to 38 cm), temperature range (24–28°C), pH (7.0–8.0), hardness, native range (Lakes Managua and Nicaragua, Nicaragua), diet, breeding, behaviour, and species-distinguishing notes (fleshy lips vs A. citrinellus). Used as authoritative baseline for all husbandry parameters.

additional.name: FishBase — Amphilophus labiatus
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Cichlidae, Cichliformes), native range, maximum reported size (38 cm TL), temperature tolerance, and IUCN conservation status. Confirmed Lake Managua and Lake Nicaragua as the sole native distribution.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Red Devil Cichlid Care Guide
additional.notes: Hobby-facing husbandry reference; confirmed tank size recommendations (125+ gal / ~475 L minimum), single-specimen keeping, aggressive temperament, diet notes, and Flowerhorn parentage (alongside A. citrinellus). Tank minimum raised to 500 L per SeriouslyFish and species size data.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Red Devil profile
additional.notes: Confirmed specialist-only classification, single-specimen tank requirement, nuchal hump development in males, and lip hypertrophy as distinguishing character from Midas Cichlid. Additional corroboration of 500 L minimum and aggressive temperament rating.

additional.name: Cichlid Room Companion — Amphilophus labiatus
additional.notes: Specialist cichlid database; provided biotope detail (rocky lakeshores, benthic zones of volcanic lakes), breeding behaviour (substrate spawning, biparental care, clutch size up to ~700 eggs), and taxonomic context within the Midas cichlid species complex.

fish.breedingNotes: Biparental substrate spawner; pair bond can be volatile — male may kill female outside spawning condition. Clutch up to ~700 adhesive eggs on flat rock. Fry free-swimming ~4–5 days post-hatch; raise on baby brine shrimp. Separate pair promptly post-independence.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Reaches 38cm; extremely aggressive Central American cichlid. Single-fish species-only tank required. Requires 500L+. Hybridizes with related Midas Cichlid (fw-fish-409) creating Flowerhorn lineage.
